_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.fossil: WTI crude is priced at approximately $71.87 per barrel, Brent crude at $71.59 per barrel, and US regular gasoline at $3.83 per gallon as of June 2026. Henry Hub natural gas is at $3.33 per MMBtu and is considered the most volatile of the three fuels when indexed to 2019 as 100; it spiked sharply in 2022 before retreating, unlike crude and gasoline which showed more parallel movements. This volatility in natural gas prices reflects supply-demand imbalances and geopolitical factors that affect gas markets distinctly compared to oil and refined products.
notes.transition: The levelised cost of electricity, which represents the average cost per megawatt-hour (MWh) of building and operating a generating plant over its lifecycle, shows solar PV at $44/MWh and onshore wind at $33/MWh. These are now cheaper than new coal and gas plants, which are $110/MWh and $76/MWh respectively, with offshore wind at $75/MWh. Solar PV’s cost has declined by about 90% since 2010, dropping from roughly $460/MWh to $44/MWh, illustrating significant technological and efficiency gains in renewable energy.
notes.currency: An estimated 20% of global oil trade is now settled outside the US dollar, a share that is rising though remains unofficial. Despite this shift, the US dollar continues to dominate oil transactions globally. The movement away from the dollar is mainly driven by Russia’s increasing trade with Asian buyers, with settlements increasingly made in yuan and rupees, reflecting geopolitical realignments and currency diversification trends in energy markets.
notes.now: Current energy markets reflect a complex interplay of fossil fuel pricing, cost-competitive renewables, and evolving currency dynamics in oil trade. While natural gas shows higher volatility in price relative to crude and gasoline, renewable electricity sources present materially lower levelised costs than new fossil generation. Simultaneously, oil trade is gradually diversifying currency usage beyond the dollar, though the US currency firmly remains central in global energy commerce.
